healthMetrics.cigMethodologyNote: Berkeley Earth conversion: 22.0 µg/m³ PM2.5 ≈ 1 cigarette/day. This compares population-level mortality risk, not individual clinical outcomes. Acute vs chronic exposure differs significantly.
healthMetrics.aqliMethodologyNote: AQLI methodology: each 10 µg/m³ PM2.5 above WHO baseline (5.0 µg/m³) ≈ 0.98 years of life expectancy lost. Calculated from annual mean PM2.5 (long-term exposure), not annual median.

seasonalEvents.cause: Agricultural burning in northern Thailand and neighbouring Mekong countries
Rain washes particulates from the air
Temperature inversion traps vehicle and industrial emissions

methodology.cigaretteEquivalence: Berkeley Earth conversion: 22.0 µg/m³ PM2.5 ≈ 1 cigarette/day. This compares population-level mortality risk, not individual clinical outcomes. Acute vs chronic exposure differs significantly.
methodology.aqli: AQLI methodology: each 10 µg/m³ PM2.5 above WHO baseline (5.0 µg/m³) ≈ 0.98 years of life expectancy lost. Calculated from annual mean PM2.5 (long-term exposure), not annual median.
methodology.dataSource: OpenAQ API v3 /sensors/{id}/days pre-computed daily aggregates

section3.title: Two Forces, One Bad Season
section3.narrative: Bangkok's worst air comes from two overlapping phenomena. From November through February, temperature inversions settle over the Chao Phraya basin—cool, still air traps emissions from millions of vehicles and industry under a low ceiling. From February through April, agricultural burning across northern Thailand and the wider Mekong sub-region drifts south, adding a layer of smoke. The overlap months—February and March—combine both. The January–April corridor sees medians from 23 to 38 µg/m³, pushing even healthy adults past safe exercise thresholds on the worst days.

section5.narrative: Bangkok splits into two cities across the year. January's peak haze—38 µg/m³ median—delivers the lung burden of nearly two cigarettes a day. Step into July's monsoon rains and the same streets drop to 11 µg/m³: clean enough that a morning run feels genuinely different. That 3.4× swing is structural, not random—it's the monsoon arriving each June with remarkable consistency. Bangkok's seasonal pattern is one of the most predictable in Southeast Asia. You can plan around it.

section6.title: Your Breathing Window
section6.intro: Bangkok's seasonal swing is reliable enough to plan a trip around. The monsoon scrubs the air clean from June onward; the haze builds just as reliably from November. Here is how to read the calendar.

bestWindow.detail: Monsoon rains continuously wash particulates from the air. July median is 11 µg/m³—WHO Good territory. August and September are nearly as clean. All outdoor activities including jogging are safe on virtually every day.

caution.detail: Transitional months with variable air quality. May sees the monsoon arriving—mostly fine with some lingering haze days. October and November mark its retreat: air quality deteriorates week by week as the dry season builds.

highRisk.detail: Peak haze season. January is the worst month at 38 µg/m³ median. Avoid prolonged outdoor exercise on hazy days. Check daily readings and use an N95 mask if PM2.5 exceeds 50 µg/m³.
